name: Akua
description: In Hawaiian mythology, Akua is a general term for gods, goddesses, spirits, and divine beings. Akua represents the supernatural forces that permeate the Hawaiian universe, embodying both benevolent and malevolent powers. They are responsible for creation, destruction, and the maintenance of balance in the natural world. Akua can manifest in various forms, including human, animal, plant, or even inanimate objects. They are often associated with specific elements, such as the ocean (represented by gods like Kanaloa) or the volcano (represented by Pele). Respect and reverence for Akua are central to Hawaiian culture, as they are believed to influence every aspect of life, from health and prosperity to success in fishing and agriculture. Offerings and prayers are traditionally given to Akua to seek their favor and avoid their wrath. The concept of Akua is deeply intertwined with the understanding of mana, the spiritual power that flows through all things, connecting the earthly realm to the divine.
subtitle: Hawaiian Gods, Spirits, and Divine Beings
appearance: Akua do not have a fixed appearance, as they can manifest in many forms. They may appear as humans, often with supernatural beauty or power radiating from them. They can also take the form of animals, such as sharks, owls, or pigs, or even natural phenomena like volcanoes or rainbows. In some cases, they may be invisible, felt only through their influence on the world.
habitat: Akua reside throughout the Hawaiian islands, inhabiting the mountains, forests, oceans, and skies. They are also present in sacred places like heiau (temples) and natural landmarks, such as waterfalls and caves. Their presence is felt most strongly in areas where the natural world is powerful and untamed.
behavior: The behavior of Akua varies greatly depending on their individual personalities and domains. Some are benevolent and generous, offering guidance and protection to humans. Others are capricious and demanding, requiring constant reverence and offerings to avoid their wrath. They are often involved in the affairs of humans, influencing their lives and testing their loyalty. Their actions reflect the interconnectedness of the natural and supernatural worlds.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Islands
keyMyths.description: The Akua Kane, Ku, and Lono are said to have created the Hawaiian Islands. Kane provided the light and life, Ku the strength and structure, and Lono the fertility and peace.

keyMyths.title: Pele and the Volcano
keyMyths.description: Pele, the goddess of volcanoes, embodies the power and destructive force of nature. Her fiery temper and control over volcanic eruptions are a testament to the Akua's power.

sources.author: Martha Warren Beckwith
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.

sources.author: Herb Kawainui Kāne
